coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of g-C3N4/BiPO4 hybrid materials via a reactable ionic liquid for the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic ciprofloxacin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Xia, Jiexiang
Zhao, Junze
Chen, Jun
Di, Jun
Ji, Mengxia
Xu, Li
Chen, Zhigang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: The novel g-C3N4/BiPO4 hybrid materials have been synthesized in reactive ionic liquid system via a facile solvothermal process. During the process, the ionic liquid not only plays an important role of solvent, dispersing agent and reactant synchronously in the synthesis of BiPO4 crystal, but also as reactive bridge for the formation of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites with the hydrogen bond acting force. The g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites showed higher photocatalytic performance for the removal of methylene blue (MB) and antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP) after the modification of g-C3N4. The results further indicated the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalyst contribute to the separation efficiency of the photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+). As a result, there was synergy role between BiPO4 and g-C3N4 in the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites, which played a significant role in improving the photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
BiPO4
Photocatalytic
Ionic liquids
Ciprofloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhancement of the photocatalytic activity via g-C3N4 modified BiPO4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites with different g-C3N4 contents.
FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite samples with different g-C3N4 contents.
XPS survey spectrum of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 (a) and its high-resolution XPS spectra: (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) P 2p, (E) C 1s and (f) N 1s.
(a) TEM image of pure BiPO4 nanostructure; (b) TEM image of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites; (c) HRTEM image of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites. (d) EDS of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB in the presence of pure BiPO4, pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/BiPO4 materials; (b) photocatalytic degradation of CIP in the presence of pure BiPO4, pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/BiPO4 materials; (c) Kinetic fit for the degradation of MB with the pure BiPO4, pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/BiPO4 materials. (d) Pseudo-first-order rate constant for MB photocatalytic oxidation under different photocatalysts.
UV–vis DRS of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite samples with different g-C3N4 contents.
Radical trapping experiments of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite.
Photoluminescence spectra of pure BiPO4 and g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of BiPO4 and 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite.
The schematic diagram of MB and CIP photodegradation over BiPO4/g-C3N4 composite photocatalysts under UV-light illumination.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of g-C3N4/BiPO4 hybrid materials via a reactable ionic liquid for the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic ciprofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel-type g-C3N4/BiPO4 hybrid materials have been synthesized in reactive ionic liquid system via a facile solvothermal method. During the process, the ionic liquid not only plays an important role of solvent, dispersing agent and reactant synchronously in the synthesis of BiPO4 crystal, but also as reactive bridge for the formation of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites with the hydrogen bond acting force. The g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites showed higher photocatalytic performance for the removal of methylene blue (MB) and antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP) after the modification of g-C3N4. The results further indicated the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalyst contribute to the separation efficiency of the photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+).

The novel g-C3N4/BiPO4 hybrid materials have been synthesized in reactive ionic liquid system via a facile solvothermal process. During the process, the ionic liquid not only plays an important role of solvent, dispersing agent and reactant synchronously in the synthesis of BiPO4 crystal, but also as reactive bridge for the formation of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites with the hydrogen bond acting force. The g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites showed higher photocatalytic performance for the removal of methylene blue (MB) and antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP) after the modification of g-C3N4. The results further indicated the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalyst contribute to the separation efficiency of the photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+). As a result, there was synergy role between BiPO4 and g-C3N4 in the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites, which played a significant role in improving the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysts have been widely employed for removing environmental pollutants and resolving energy shortage in recent years [1–3]. TiO2 as one of the attractive UV photocatalysts, shows high photocatalytic activity, great chemical stability and low cost in the photocatalytic decomposition of organic pollutants [4–6]. However, for TiO2 material, large band gap of 3.2eV and low separation rate of the photo-excited electron-hole pairs are the main two shortcomings, which hinder its practical application. Considering the above reasons, great deal of researches have been conducted to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 under UV irradiation, which contained coupling with other semiconductors, metallic doping, non-metallic doping and sensitization [7,8]. Meanwhile, novel types of semiconductor photocatalysts such as Bi2MoO6 [9], g-C3N4 [10] and BiOBr [11] have been exploited by varieties of methods in order to expand the light absorption range of the catalyst and enhance its photocatalytic properties.

Recently, BiPO4 has been widely employed as photocatalyst due to its high photocatalytic activity for the degradation of different types organic pollutants under UV-light irradiation [12–16]. Efficient photocatalytic activity of BiPO4 materials were attributed to the high separation efficiency of the photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+), which generated by the intrinsic induced effect of oxyacid anions containing high negative charges. Simultaneously, the deactivation of BiPO4 has not been presented during the photocatalytic processes. Due to such excellent advantages over TiO2 (P25), many design and researches on BiPO4 crystal has been further carried out in the field of photocatalysis [17]. Our previous work has prepared BiPO4 nanorods with different morphologies via a solvothermal process in the presence of [Omim]H2PO4 ionic liquid in H2O, ethanol, ethylene glycol (EG) and ethylene glycol monomethyl ether (EGME), respectively. The ionic liquid plays a part of solvent, template and reactant synchronously in the process of reaction [18]. Moreover, on account of the quantum efficiency of photocatalytic system can be improved by the decreased recombination efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, semiconductor coupling has been already applied to enhance the phototcatalytic activity [19]. For example, BiOI/Ag3VO4 [20], SnS2/g-C3N4 [21] and BiPO4/BiOI [22], which are composite semiconductor photocatalysts, have been capable to advance the photocatalytic efficiency in degradation of organic dyes under light irradiation.

Graphite-like carbon nitride materials (g-C3N4) have attracted much scientific interest in a variety of photocatalysis, such as water splitting and organic pollutant degradation due to the nontoxic, economical, good thermal and chemical stability and easy of synthesis [23–25]. Therefore, g-C3N4 as a metal-free catalyst is identified to be a “sustainable develop” photocatalyst. In spite of this, since the high recombination efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, small surface area and lack of light absorption, the photocatalytic activity of pure g-C3N4 is poor. Based on these drawbacks, it is vital to adopt efficient strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, such as combining g-C3N4 with semiconductor materials to construct heterojunction. These researches showed that the g-C3N4 can be utilized as an auxiliary catalyst to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of semiconductors in degradation of organic dyes. Moreover, the layered structure of g-C3N4 is advantage to the transfer of electrons. Our group has previously reported g-C3N4/AgI [26], g-C3N4/BiOBr [27], g-C3N4/BiOI [28] and Bi4O5I2/g-C3N4 [29] and so on. The results proved that g-C3N4 modification can strengthen photocatalytic efficiency for degradation of organic dyes. Meanwhile, the sufficient interface contact between g-C3N4 and semiconductor is beneficial to the separation and transfer of photo-generated charge carries.

Herein, g-C3N4 modified BiPO4 photocatalyst has been prepared in ionic liquid system via a facile solvothermal method. During the reaction, ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 could improve the dispersion of g-C3N4. At the same time, the cationic 1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium in the [Omim]H2PO4 possesses positive polarity, which could combine together with the g-C3N4 and make the g-C3N4 and BiPO4 construct a tight connection, increasing the separation of photo-genrated charges. In this paper, the ionic liquid played a part of solvent, dispersing agent and reactant synchronously in the formation of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites. The results indicated that the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite has remarkably increased for the degradation of CIP, owing to the improved separation efficiency of the photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+). In addition, the structure-activity relation of the g-C3N4/BiPO4 photocatalysts and the possible photocatalytic mechanism has been studied in detail.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. The ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 (1-decyl-3-methylimidazolium dihydric phosphate) (97%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd. The g-C3N4 was prepared originated from the literature [30].

g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites were synthesized through a facile solvothermal method. In the first of experimental process, 3.5mmol ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 was added into 20ml ethylene glycol (EG) solution and made it completely dissolved. In the second of experimental process, then the as-prepared g-C3N4 and 1mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were added in the above solution. All mixture kept stirring for 30min at room temperature, then diverted into 25ml Tenflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 160°C for 24h. Finally, the samples were naturally cooled down to room temperature and collected by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and ethanol for four times, then dried at 60°C in air. The g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites with different mass ratios of C3N4 to BiPO4 (5%, 10% and 15%) were synthesized according to the above method and the corresponding samples were flagged as 5wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4, 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 and 15wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4, respectively.
For comparison, BiPO4 samples were also prepared with the same method using [Omim]H2PO4 ionic liquid.

The crystalline structure of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites were characterized by utilizing X-ray diffraction (Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer) using Cu Kα (λ =1.5418Å) radiation with a scanning speed of 7°min−1 from 10° to 90°. A high-resoultion transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) micrographs were collected on a JEOL-JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) operating of 200KV. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) presented the optical diffuse reflectance spectra of products by using Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800nm with the BaSO4 as a white standard reference. The recognition and analysis of molecular construction of the products were obtained on a Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Nexus 470, Thermo Electron Corporation) with KBr disks at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on an ESCALab MKII X-ray photo-electron spectrometer using the Mg Kα radiation. The measurements above were performed at room temperature.

The photocatalytic activities of the g-C3N4/BiPO4 as-synthesized photocatalyst were measured on the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) under UV light irradiation with a 250W high pressure Hg lamp. 30mg g-C3N4/BiPO4 product was dispersed into 100ml of MB or CIP (10mg/l) aqueous solution in a quartz reactor. During the photocatalytic reaction process, aeration and magnetic stirring were sustained to make sure that adequate oxygen can be fully promoted the mixing of photocatalysts and solution. The suspension was magnetically stirred for 30min in dark to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. At every given time period, a handful of suspension (about 3ml) was taken out from the reaction cell and separated by a centrifuge at a speed of 13,000rpm for 3min to wipe off the samples inside. The concentration of MB or CIP solution was analyzed through an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2450) at the absorption band maximum (664nm for MB and 276nm for CIP).

XRD was used to determine the crystalline structure of the as-prepared g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 1 , the diffraction planes of pure BiPO4 at 19.03°, 21.34°, 25.21°, 27.16°, 29.10°, 31.26° and 34.52° were assigned to the (011), (−111), (111), (200), (120), (012) and (−202) lattice plane, respectively [31]. All the diffraction peaks can be indexed to the monoclinic phase of BiPO4, corresponding to the standard pattern (JCPDS 15-0767). The pure g-C3N4 presents two distinct peaks at 2θ=13.1°and 2θ=27.3°, which were readily indexed to the (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4 standard pattern (JCPDS 87-1526). However, with regard to the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite, no typical peaks of g-C3N4 crystal were detected in the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite. It may be due to its low concentration and well dispersed of g-C3N4 in the composite photocatalysts. Besides that, no other crystalline phase can be detected, demonstrating the high purity of g-C3N4/BiPO4 samples.

FT-IR spectroscopy of the as-synthesized g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts is depicted in Fig. 2 to further ascertain the presence of g-C3N4. For the pure BiPO4 samples, four characteristic intense spectrums centered at 1073cm−1, 1010cm−1, 953cm−1 and 925cm−1 correspond to the v3 stretching vibrataion of the phosphate (PO4) group [32]. The peaks at 1620cm−1 belong to the O-H groups bending vibration of the water adsorbed in the surface of the products.. In contrast with the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite products, it can be clearly observed other characteristic peaks, which are ascribed to the typical peaks of g-C3N4. The intense band at 810cm−1 is attributed to the classic breathing mode of s-triazine. Additionally, the characteristic spectrums at 1567, and 1406cm−1 are also discovered, belonging to the representative stretching modes of CN hetero-cycles [33]. Moreover, the FT-IR intensity peaks of g-C3N4 increase with the increasing content of the g-C3N4. The entire FT-IR spectra of BiPO4 and g-C3N4 are well detected, indicating the two components are of harmonious coexistence. There is no absorption band of ionic liquid in the FT-IR spectra, demonstrating that the ionic liquids are simply removed.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to confirm the surface chemical composition of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites. Fig. 3 a presents the peaks of Bi 4p, Bi 4d, Bi 5d, Bi 4f, O 1s, O 2s, C 1s, N 1s, P 2p, which was corresponding to the chemical composition of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts. In addition, the high resolution XPS spectra of the Bi 4f, O 1s, P 2p, C 1s and N 1s regions for the as-synthesized samples were shown in Fig. 3(b–f). It can be observed that there are two distinguished peaks around 164.9eV and 159.6eV of Bi 4f (Fig. 3b), demonstrating the Bi in the composites exhibits the oxidation state of Bi3+. A broad signal existing in O 1s region was approximately 530.9eV (Fig. 3c), which is assigned to the lattice oxygen. For the P 2p, the spectra at 132.7eV expressed the existence of a quinquevalent oxidation state for P (Fig. 3d). The major peak at 284.6eV is uniquely attributed to CC bonding (Fig. 3e). The peak located at 287.6eV was assigned to defect-containing sp2-bonded carbon (CN). Also from Fig. 2f, the typical peak of N 1s is observed at about 398.7eV and 400.6eV, which can be attributed to CNC groups and tertiary nitrogen N-(C)3 groups, respectively [34]. The XPS data gives a testimony for the coexistence of BiPO4 and g-C3N4 in the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts. And these results are consistent with the FT-IR analysis.

The morphologies and microstructures of the BiPO4 and g-C3N4/BiPO4 products were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From Fig. 4 a, the pure BiPO4 products synthesized by a facile solvothermal method are composed of nano-particles with the average length about 50nm. The precise information on the surface of BiPO4 crystals was supplied in Fig. 4b and c. After introducing g-C3N4, the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalyst displays analogous morphologies as that of pure BiPO4 (Fig. 4c). Moreover, the addition of g-C3N4 has no effect on the size of BiPO4. Remarkably, BiPO4 nanostructures have been better dispersed in the environment of ionic liquids and formed a good interfacial contact with g-C3N4. It can be convinced that the g-C3N4 and BiPO4 as the two active components of the products are physical and electronic contact. The chemical composition of composite photocatalyst was also confirmed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Fig. 4d). The EDS pattern manifests the g-C3N4/BiPO4 products contain C, N, O, P and Bi element. It further proves the composite products were pure g-C3N4/BiPO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of the photocatalytic activity via g-C3N4 modified BiPO4
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic properties of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites with different content of g-C3N4 as well as pure BiPO4 were test by the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) under UV light illumination, as shown in Fig. 5 . C represents the concentration of MB or CIP after UV illumination, as well as C0 represents the concentration after adsorption equilibrium in the absence of UV-light. During the photocatalysis, blank tests (MB or CIP solution without any photocatalyst) confirmed the self-photolysis of MB or CIP could be ignored, exhibiting little photolysis under UV-light illumination. It also could be seen that MB or CIP was gradually decomposed by the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts with the increase of UV-light illumination time. Fig. 5a showed the degradation rate of MB is 62.0%, 70.8%, 89.8%, 78.4%, 17.3% for pure BiPO4, 5wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4, 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4, 15wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 and pure g-C3N4, respectively. The g-C3N4/BiPO4 displayed much higher activity for the removal of MB than pure BiPO4 and g-C3N4. The 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 sample exhibited the best activity. In addition, from Fig. 5b, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/BiPO4 was also much higher than the pure BiPO4 and g-C3N4 for the degradation of CIP. And 96.6% of CIP can be removed by the optimal 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 sample. What is more, the photocatalytic activity is decreased with higher g-C3N4 content. Thus, there has synergy role between BiPO4 and g-C3N4 in the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites, which played a significant role in improving the photocatalytic activity. The reason and mechanism of increasing photoactivity was further studied by the DRS, PL, EIS and radical trapping experiments.

The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of MB by g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites were evaluated, and the results were shown in Fig. 5c. It was found that the photocatalytic degradation process followed pseudo-first-order kinetics by the equation of −ln (C/C0)= kt, where k is the first-order reaction rate constant, C0 is the absorption equilibrium concentration of MB, C is the concentration of MB at irradiation time, and t is the irradiation time. The greater k is, the faster reaction rate is. From Fig. 5c, we can observed the first-order reaction rate constant k is counted as 0.0064, 0.0089, 0.0167, 0.0106 and 0.0103min−1 for pure BiPO4, 5wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4, 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4, 15wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 and pure g-C3N4, respectively. That is to say, the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites have greater photocatalytic activity than that of pure BiPO4 and g-C3N4. Clearly, the photoactivity of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite was 2.6 times higher than that of pure BiPO4, and 12.8 times higher than that of g-C3N4. It is evidenced that the g-C3N4 modified BiPO4 possess a remarkable synergistic effect in the process of increasing the photocatalytic efficiency. At the same time, the pseudo-first-order constants and relative coefficients are summarized in Fig. 5d.

The photophysical properties of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite samples were measured by UV/vis absorption spectra. Fig. 6 shows a comparison of the UV/vis absorption spectra for the pure BiPO4 and g-C3N4/BiPO4. According to the spectra, the absorption edge of pure BiPO4 occurred at approximately 300nm, which is consistent with the large band gap 3.98eV [35]. After g-C3N4 contents were introduced, the absorption intensity of the g-C3N4/BiPO4 in the UV-light region was memorably enhanced, and the absorption began to rise with the addition of g-C3N4 contents. Correspondingly, a slight red shift appeared. The enhanced light-trapping of the composited photocatalyst g-C3N4/BiPO4 in the UV-light may lead to forming more electron-hole pairs, which further brings out a higher photo-activity under UV-light irradiation [36].

As we all known, the oxidative species of the photocatalytic reaction are of great significant for illustrating the photocatalytic mechanism. The radical trapping experiments can discover the oxidative species during the photocatalytic reaction. The active species in our photocatalytic process for the oxidization of organic pollutants could be verified by hole, superoxide and hydroxyl radical trapping experiments. As shown in Fig. 7 , the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites was significantly suppressed by the addition of EDTA-2Na as the scavenger for holes. Besides this, the addition of BQ as the scavenger for superoxide radical showed some inhibitory effect on the photoactivity of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites. On the contrary, the addition of IPA as the scavenger for hydroxyl radical did not drastically changes the photodegration of organic pollutants. Therefore, the result demonstrated that the photo-generated holes and superoxide radical were the main oxidative species for the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites during the photocatalytic oxidization of organic pollutants. No role for hydroxyl radical had been played in the photocatalytic reaction.

To further inspect the influence of g-C3N4 modification, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite samples were carried out. The migration, transfer, and separation of the photo-generated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) in semiconductor materials were disclosed by PL spectra. As is displayed in Fig. 8 , the PL spectrum of the g-C3N4/BiPO4 is similar to that of pure BiPO4. It can be viewed that the PL spectrum of the g-C3N4/BiPO4 and the pure BiPO4 possess main emission wavelengths around 460nm. The PL spectrum of the 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalyst displayed a significantly weaker intensity peak than that of pure BiPO4, demonstrating that the recombination probability of photo-induced carriers in g-C3N4/BiPO4 is lower [37]. This result clearly shows that the introduction of g-C3N4 increases the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers. In other words, this may be the reason for the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts has the better photocatalytic activity than the pure BiPO4 samples.

In order to better know the reason of different photoactivity for the pure BiPO4 and g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite materials, the typical electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) responses were performed. As is shown in Fig. 9 , only one arc or semicircle appears on the EIS images, insinuating the shift of surface photo-induced charges is the rate-determining step in the photocatalytic process. The smaller the arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plot is, the higher efficiency separation of the photo-generated electrons and holes is, and vice versa [38,39]. Fig. 9 showed that the arc radius of 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts was smaller than that of pure BiPO4 in dark, implying that 10wt% g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts had smaller electric resistance and higher transfer efficiency of charges than pure BiPO4. As a consequence, the coupling of g-C3N4 with BiPO4 was in favor of the separation and migration of charges, increasing the photocatalytic activity [40].

On the basis of the EIS, radical trapping experiments and PL analysis, a possible mechanism of the photocatalytic efficiency for degradation of pollutants by g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalyst is put forward.

The g-C3N4 modified BiPO4 composite photocatalyst played an important role in the highly efficient separation of photo-generated electrons and holes during the photocatalysis reaction. In order to verify the photocatalytic mechanism, we provide the energy band structures of BiPO4 and g-C3N4. The band position of BiPO4 and g-C3N4 can be estimated according to the following empirical formulas [41]:
(1) E VB
= X − Ee
+0.5Eg
(2) E CB
=E VB
− Eg
Where X is the electro-negativity of the semiconductor, in which the X value BiPO4 are 6.49eV. Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5eV), and Eg is the band gap energy of the semiconductor. The E VB and E CB are the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) potentials, respectively. Thus, the E VB and E CB of BiPO4 were calculated as 3.98eV and 0eV, respectively. At the same time, the E VB and E CB of g-C3N4 were separately calculated as 1.17eV and −0.73eV.

BiPO4 and g-C3N4 semiconductor materials closely combined together when the g-C3N4 was introduced into BiPO4 nanostructure. It is considered to be that the closed interface combination of BiPO4/g-C3N4 is beneficial to the transfer of the photo-generated holes in the VB of BiPO4 to the g-C3N4 under UV-light illumination, and transfer the electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 to BiPO4, as is presented in Fig. 10 . In such a situation, the separation of photo-generated electrons and holes was enhanced, which is in accord with the result of the EIS and PL analysis. The electrons as great reductant in BiPO4 nanostructure can capture the O2 adsorbed on the composite materials and then reduce it to ·O2−. Furthermore, the pollutants was decomposed by the ·O2−. At the same time, the pollutants could be oxidized by the holes in the VB of C3N4 directly. Based on the above results, the introduction of g-C3N4 plays a significant role in the photocatalytic activity.

BiPO4 was modified by different g-C3N4 contents in ionic liquid via a facile solvothermal method. The enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/BiPO4 composite photocatalysts for the degradation of MB and CIP under UV-light illumination was assigned to the highly efficient separation and movement of photogenerated charges. The BiPO4 modified by 10wt% g-C3N4 contents shows the highest photocatalytic performance. There has synergy role between BiPO4 and g-C3N4 in the g-C3N4/BiPO4 composites. The main reactive species were determined to be the holes and ·O2− by the trapping experiments. The closed interface combination of BiPO4/g-C3N4 is beneficial to inhibit the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes and promote the interfacial charges transfer. As a result, the metal-free g-C3N4 modified BiPO4 hybrid material is a novel-type and promising photocatlytic material, exhibiting potential applications in the field of environmental purifications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576123, 21476098 and 21471069), and the Science and Technology support program of Zhenjiang(SH2014018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spatial separation of photogenerated electrons and holes among {010} and {110} crystal facets of BiVO4
